metadata.title: Taking stock and moving forward: A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job insecurity research

abstract: We collected the abstracts of manuscripts examining job insecurity published between 1984 and 2019 and carried out a textual analysis to investigate the defining clusters, their development over time, and whether there was evidence of siloed knowledge. Results suggested that job insecurity research seems to be fragmented into disciplinary foci (organizational psychology, public health, economics, and sociology). Further analyses on the organizational psychology corpus, revealed 25 topics with distinct temporal trajectories: some were increasing (analytical advances and differentiation between cognitive and affective job insecurity) while other were decreasing (scale development). The remaining abstracts revealed 15 topics with more stable trajectories. Based on these results, we identified five areas for future organizational research on job insecurity: the changing labor market, the need to better understand the experiences of marginalized workers and non-work outcomes of job insecurity, the added-value of qualitative research, and the need to critically evaluate our assumptions as researchers.

paragraphs: While the above quote is typically applied to individual or societal development and progress, it is equally germane to the advancement of scientific inquiry. For a discipline to progress, it must first know where it has been.

Although researchers have many tools at their disposal to aggregate the knowledge accrued within a discipline (e.g., meta-analysis), these are often done for the purpose of answering a pre-defined research question, i.e., is X related to Y and/or does this relationship differ by Z? Such reviews are not usually intended to describe the breadth of research questions being asked (and by whom) or to track the rise and fall trajectories of those topics over time. Yet, understanding where a field has been and how it has developed historically can give us valuable insights into the strengths and weaknesses of our scientific approaches and suggest a path forward for fruitful future research. Toward that end and in keeping with recent calls for new methodologies to synthesize knowledge in organizational psychology (e.g., Cronin & Homan, 2020), the purpose of our study is to advance job insecurity research by using textual statistical analysis to provide a more holistic view of research across different domains and how it has evolved over time with the goal of breaking down the siloes that may exist between them. In doing so, we make several important contributions to the field: first, by providing a bird's eye review, we can raise awareness among scholars of the rich body of job insecurity research across multiple disciplines. Second, by synthesizing and integrating the knowledge from different disciplines, we highlight opportunities for scholars to bridge these siloes. We believe that doing so can contribute to accelerating and enriching the job insecurity research agenda to keep pace with the evolving economic scenario that is increasingly dominated by crises and downturns. Third, our review provides a common framework that can be used by researchers from different discipline to understand what colleagues in other fields are focusing on, facilitating dialogue and collaboration.
Hence, we aim to answer the following questions:

1. What are the defining clusters of job insecurity research? What themes, disciplinary approaches, and/or scientific methods do they represent? 2. How have the trajectories of these clusters changed over time? Have certain clusters stagnated or diminished over time, whereas others have flourished? 3. Are the emergent clusters suggestive of fragmented or siloed approaches to job insecurity research? Conversely, what are areas of commonalities in terms of research foci and/or methodological approaches?

In answering these questions, we hope to better understand how research on job insecurity has progressed within organizational psychology 1 , as well as in related disciplines, and what our history (and any identified gaps) might imply regarding how best the field can progress in the future with respect to theoretical, methodological, and empirical developments.

Our paper is organized as follows: we first discuss the variety of ways that research can be synthesized and consider some of the unique advantages of textual analysis. We then provide a brief review of the origins of job insecurity research, the disciplines that have contributed to this field, and different approaches to conceptualizing job insecurity. Next, we discuss the scope of literature considered in this review and the rationale for our chosen corpus. We then use two complementary algorithms to create research clusters and track their development over time. Finally, we provide a summary of unique insights that emerged and identify paths forward.

sections.paper_id: Bazzoli and Probst - 2022 - Taking stock and moving forward A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job i
paragraphs: Academic knowledge can be synthesized in different ways. Meta-analyses (see e.g., Jiang & Lavaysse, 2018), systematic reviews (see e.g., De Witte et al., 2016), narrative reviews (see e.g., Schaufeli, 2016;Shoss, 2017;Sinclair et al., 2021), meta-syntheses (see e.g., Bazzoli & Probst, 2022a), and bibliometric analyses (see e.g., Prado-Gascó et al., 2021) have recently been used to summarize job insecurity knowledge. Each one of them has benefits and drawbacks. For instance, meta-analyses provide indices of covariation and effect sizes, but these are ahistorical and acontextual. Narrative and systematic reviews offer a theoretical model that can inform future research but those come from scholars that have their own biases. Meta-syntheses summarize qualitative research but are somewhat limited in scope. Finally, citation analyses can identify clusters but rely on papers' citation count, which has been found to be flawed and not indicative of a paper's quality.

On the other hand, textual statistical analysis can facilitate the identification of themes, processes, theoretical foundations, and methods used by job insecurity researchers by statistically classifying a body of work (called a "corpus") into topical clusters, as well as mapping the trajectories of these clusters over time. The primary advantages gained from taking a textual statistical approach are twofold. First, these approaches can ease the cognitive burden on the researcher by providing them with an initial algorithmically-derived set of manageable classes. Second, textual statistics complement findings from meta-analyses as well as narrative and systematic reviews. Whereas meta-analyses are limited to providing an effect size for a specific relationship, systematic and narrative reviews often propose a theoretical model to be tested. Textual statistics adds to these approaches by algorithmically facilitating the identification of underlying themes and crossing of disciplinary boundaries by tracing the trajectories of meaningful clusters of knowledge, themes and methodological approaches investigated over an extended period of time. In sum, our analysis links scholarly work across different theories, domains, and disciplines that broadly relate to job insecurity in order to make new theoretical contributions by summarizing and systematizing the empirical research.

sections.paper_id: Bazzoli and Probst - 2022 - Taking stock and moving forward A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job i
sections.header_raw: Origins of job insecurity research and approaches to conceptualizing job insecurity
sections.header_norm: origins of job insecurity research and approaches to conceptualizing job insecurity
paragraphs: Concerted attention among organizational scholars on the topic of job insecurity arguably began with the publication of Greenhalgh and Rosenblatt's (1984) seminal article appearing in Academy of Management Review. In that article, they proposed a theoretical model of the causes, nature, effects, and organizational outcomes of job insecurity, as well as potential moderators of those relationships. Prior to their proposed model, only a handful of peerreviewed journal articles had focused on the topic of job insecurity. Yet, subsequent to their publication, research interest grew exponentially with thousands of articles appearing in organizational psychology outlets (e.g., the special issue on job insecurity edited by Klandermans & van Vuuren, 1999) and expanding to other disciplinary literatures including public health, economics, and sociology (e.g., Benach et al., 2014;Burgard, 2020).

In their pivotal article, Greenhalgh and Rosenblatt (1984) defined job insecurity as "perceived powerlessness to maintain desired continuity in a threatened job situation " (p. 438). This definition spurred the development of a multidimensional measure of job insecurity (Ashford et al., 1989) that operationalized job insecurity by assessing: 1) the range of work situation features that could be in jeopardy, 2) the valence of each feature, 3) the subjective probability of losing each feature, and 4) perceived powerlessness to counter threats to one's job. However, researchers (e.g., Probst, 2003) subsequently argued that there were drawbacks to such a conceptualization, noting the importance of clearly distinguishing aspects of the construct itself (i.e., perceived threat to one's job) from variables (such as powerlessness or desired continuity) that may moderate the relationships between the construct and outcome variables of interest.

In the years following the Greenhalgh and Rosenblatt (1984) article, other areas of debate among scholars within and outside of organizational psychology included: (i) whether job insecurity is best conceptualized as a subjective experience best viewed from the "eye of the beholder" or an objective state defined by demonstrable threats to one's position such as impending layoffs (e.g., De Witte & Näswall, 2003;Klandermans et al., 2010); (ii) whether job insecurity is a unidimensional (e.g., Johnson et al., 1984;Mohr, 2000;van Vuuren, 1990) or multidimensional construct (e.g., Ashford et al., 1989;Greenhalgh & Rosenblatt, 1984;Hartley & Klandermans, 1986;Roskies & Louis-Guerin, 1990); (iii) whether job insecurity should distinguish threatened job loss (so-called "quantitative job insecurity") from threats to valued aspects of one's job (i.e., "qualitative job insecurity"; Hellgren et al., 1999); and, (iv) whether it is theoretically or practically meaningful to differentiate between cognitive and affective aspects of job insecurity (e.g., Bazzoli & Probst, 2022b;Borg & Elizur, 1992;Pienaar et al., 2013;Probst, 2003).

Recently, Shoss (2017) sought to bridge these different perspectives by defining job insecurity as a "perceived threat to the continuity and stability of employment as it is currently experienced (p. 1914)." Thus, this definition takes a global view of job insecurity while acknowledging that perceived threats can occur due to potential job loss as well as deteriorating job conditions. Shoss stands firmly with the viewpoint that job insecurity is subjectively experienced, while acknowledging that one's experience can be influenced by objective states such as holding a temporary position or facing organizational downsizing. Finally, this perspective reflects that job insecurity is first and foremost a cognitive perception, but still acknowledges that affective reactions to that perceived threat (i.e., affective job insecurity) are important proximal outcomes.

sections.paper_id: Bazzoli and Probst - 2022 - Taking stock and moving forward A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job i
paragraphs: In considering the scope of our textual statistics synthesis of job insecurity research, we considered the pros and cons associated with taking a narrower organizational psychology historical focus versus a broader approach that acknowledges the contributions and insights to be gained from other disciplinary inquiries into this topic. On the one hand, prominent scholars and leaders within the organizational psychology fields have repeatedly cautioned against disciplinary ethnocentrism (Campbell, 1969) and self-constructed silos (DeNisi, 2010) stemming from an introverted selfabsorption with purely management-related issues (Suddaby, 2012). Collectively and repeatedly, such scholars have advocated for an interdisciplinary approach so as to avoid fragmentation and balkanization of our knowledge. Heeding these warnings, there may be value in assessing the extent to which research into the topic of job insecurity exhibits these siloed tendencies.

On the other hand, readers of Organizational Psychology Review may be most interested in better understanding the gaps in and trajectories of research within the specific discipline of organizational psychology. Moreover, as alluded to earlier, from a conceptual standpoint, the very notion of "job insecurity" among researchers within and across disciplines may have different meanings. This can potentially be characterized as not only a limitation of past research, but also a challenge of using the entire body of literature to conduct a textual statistics synthesis of prior research. Nevertheless, because we believe there is valuable information to be gained through both approaches, we first conducted our textual statistics analyses using the entire corpus of job insecurity research (regardless of disciplinary origin). Following this (and because of the immediate disciplinary branching that was observed), we subsequently conducted separate follow-up analyses on the organizational psychology corpus and the non-organizational psychology corpus to evaluate themes and trends within these distinct areas.

Collectively and repeatedly, such scholars have advocated for an interdisciplinary approach so as to avoid fragmentation and balkanization of our knowledge. Heeding these warnings, there may be value in assessing the extent to which research into the topic of job insecurity exhibits these siloed tendencies.

On the other hand, readers of Organizational Psychology Review may be most interested in better understanding the gaps in and trajectories of research within the specific discipline of organizational psychology. Moreover, as alluded to earlier, from a conceptual standpoint, the very notion of "job insecurity" among researchers within and across disciplines may have different meanings. This can potentially be characterized as not only a limitation of past research, but also a challenge of using the entire body of literature to conduct a textual statistics synthesis of prior research. Nevertheless, because we believe there is valuable information to be gained through both approaches, we first conducted our textual statistics analyses using the entire corpus of job insecurity research (regardless of disciplinary origin). Following this (and because of the immediate disciplinary branching that was observed), we subsequently conducted separate follow-up analyses on the organizational psychology corpus and the non-organizational psychology corpus to evaluate themes and trends within these distinct areas.

paragraphs: Textual statistics requires the identification of a meaningful corpus. The corpus can be as large as the contents of all full-text articles to as small as the titles of published articles. In the current study, we took a "happy medium" approach by defining the corpus as the text content of all job insecurity abstracts published within the past 40 years. 2

sections.paper_id: Bazzoli and Probst - 2022 - Taking stock and moving forward A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job i
paragraphs: In order to identify relevant papers, we conducted an initial literature search of several academic databases (EBSCO, Google Scholar, PsychINFO, PubMed, Sage, ScienceDirect, Scopus, Sociological Abstracts, SpringerLink, Web of Science) searching for abstracts that contained the words "job insecurity" or "employment insecurity" as well as "job security" and "employment security" in the title, in the abstract itself, or in the article's keywords 3 . Additionally, we searched for papers using scientific networks, reference lists from key papers, and a list of active job insecurity scholars. We approached our literature search from a discipline-blind perspective seeking published and unpublished contributions with the goals of obtaining a comprehensive and exhaustive dataset of relevant abstracts while avoiding publication bias. This process was carried out in January 2020 and resulted in 4657 non-unique entries.

To ensure that the sample of contribution used for analysis was appropriate and suitable, we used the following inclusion/exclusion criteria: (a) only contributions that appeared between January 1984 (the publication of the seminal job insecurity paper by Greenhalgh & Rosenblatt, 1984) and January 2020 were considered; (b) contributions that did not have an abstract (e.g., book chapters, editorials, errata, acknowledgements) were excluded; (c) contributions that appeared more than once across the databases were included only once; (d) the main topic of the contributions needed to be job insecurity, and (e) contributions needed to provide an English abstract (regardless of the language of the full-text). This procedure led to an overall dataset of 1449 unique abstracts in the corpus. Figure 1 depicts the screening process, consistent with PRISMA guidelines (Page et al., 2021).

paragraphs: To facilitate the textual analyses of the corpus, we next conducted several recommended preliminary steps (Kobayashi et al., 2018). First, the corpus was normalized by means of removing punctuation and transforming uppercase letters into lowercase ones. Since both American and British spelling were present in the abstracts, we converted all British spelling into American English (e.g., -ise became -ize) for consistency purposes. Second, the corpus was stemmed by grouping together all inflected forms of a word into its root (e.g., growth, growing, and grew were recoded as grow). The authors also manually checked the stemming process to ensure that inflected words with different meanings were not inadvertently grouped together (e.g., the adjective "good" and the noun "goods"). Subsequently, multi-word phrases (e.g., job insecurity, affective commitment) were recoded as a single word (i.e., job_insecurity, affective_commitment). Additionally, we screened the corpus for words that in the realm of the social sciences are functionally synonyms (e.g., moderation and interaction). Those words were grouped together under the most frequently occurring word. Last, following accepted best-practices in the field (Kobayashi et al., 2018), we deleted words and multi-word phrases that (a) occurred less than five times in the entire corpus, (b) were present in more than 80% of the abstracts, or (c) belonged to a standard "stop-list" of words (e.g., conjunctions, numbers, and prepositions) that are not of substantive interest (Boyd, 2018).

sections.header_raw: Data analysis strategy: a quali-quantitative procedure
sections.header_norm: data analysis strategy a quali quantitative procedure
paragraphs: As noted in the introduction, the main obstacle to creating a synthesis of job insecurity research that spans across the fields of organizational psychology, economics, public health, and sociology is to code a huge amount of data (i.e., the abstracts). To solve this issue, in the current study, we use two complementary computational approaches: the latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA; Blei et al., 2003;Griffiths & Steyvers, 2004) and the Reinert method (Ratinaud, 2009;Reinert, 1983). The output of such algorithm is a set of categories that were jointly analyzed by the authors following well-established qualitative procedures in order to make sense of and uncover relationships between categories. Below, we describe briefly the logical and computational steps associated with both approaches 4 ; please see Blei et al. (2003) and Ratinaud (2009) for further technical details.

LDA. LDA is an unsupervised generative probabilistic model of a corpus. The idea behind the analytical approach is that abstracts can be represented as random mixtures of latent topics, in which each latent topic can be defined by a distribution of words (Blei et al., 2003). It follows that within each topic, the words with the highest probabilities (presented at the top of the list in the results section) give a good sense of the meanings associated with that latent topic. Two Dirichlet hyperparameters need to be fixed prior conducting the analysis, as well as the desired number of topics (denoted as T). These assumptions have important implications for the results produced by the model. Thus, following previous literature (Griffiths & Steyvers, 2004), we fixed α = 50/ T and δ = 0.5 for the LDA on the full corpus and α = 50/T and δ = 0.1 for the follow-up LDAs. 5 We started from a model with T = 2, then estimated a model with T = 5, and increased T by five units thereafter, up to T = 50. We used the Gibbs algorithm for both runs. Following Bayesian theory, model selection is done by computing the posterior probability of the investigated models given the data. In this case, we aim to evaluate the model's likelihood given the data and the parameters. We used an R package (topicmodels; Grün & Hornik, 2011) to run the LDA.

We believe that being able to identify "hot" and "cold" topics (i.e., topics that showed increasing vs. decreasing scholarly interest over time) by analyzing their temporal prevalence is one of the most interesting applications of this analysis for researchers. A simple trend analysis allows the identification of these hot and cold topics across time. 6 Reinert Method. The Reinert method is based on the analysis of word co-occurrences as a means of identifying semantic classes characterized by a specific vocabulary of words associated with one another (Reinert, 1983). The clustering procedure identifies the classes by means of maximizing the χ 2 value between classes (Reinert, 1983). At the beginning of the analysis, the algorithm creates two classes by grouping words into each class so that such classes are the most consistent within them but are the most different between each other. The next iteration considers each class separately, repeating the process of creating two new classes within each class created by the previous iteration until the classes are homogenous so that any further disaggregation would be impossible. We used an R package (IRaMuTeQ; Ratinaud, 2009) to run these analyses.

The results section is organized as follows: first, we ran an LDA on the full corpus to identify latent topics that are representative of different research traditions and disciplines. We followed this analysis by implementing a Reinert analysis on the same corpus. After analyzing the topics/ classes, we assigned each abstract to its most probable topic (using the abstract's highest posterior probability to belong to a given topic from the LDA). This created two smaller corpora that were further analyzed with follow-up LDA and trend analyses.

paragraphs: Results from the full corpus LDA Approach. The corpus was comprised of 5156 unique words that occurred 269,270 times. We ran a series of LDA models with a maximum of 50 topics (in 5-unit increments) and inspected the change in resulting loglikelihood values. In line with Griffiths and Steyvers (2004), the loglikelihood curve showed that the optimal model of T = 15 is complex enough to accurately fit the information available without producing more parameters than can be justified by the data.

Table 1 depicts the topics and the top 20 representative words for each, ranked based on their highest posterior probability. Many of the identified topics reflected rather clear disciplinary boundaries. For instance, topics 3 (containing words focused on commonly studied outcomes of job insecurity, including job satisfaction, engagement, and commitment), 10 (containing words focusing on quantitative job insecurity: job loss, affect, subjective, threat, worry), and 14 (containing words commonly studied in relation to qualitative job insecurity, e.g., benefit, career, time, valued aspects) were related largely to organizational psychology. Topics 4 and 7 represented occupational medicine with clear foci on physical health outcomes of job insecurity (e.g., blood pressure, depression, cardiac disease). Topics 2 (e.g., wellbeing, life satisfaction, work-family conflict, partner, child, care) and 6 (e.g., burnout, safety, injury, accident underreporting) contained constructs (e.g., work-life balance and occupational safety) germane to the narrower sub-discipline of occupational health psychology. Topic 8 reflected research in macroeconomics with emerging key words such as labor market, wage, public/private, and economy; on the other hand, Topic 9 represented the microeconomic approach (e.g., with a focus on the nature of the permanent vs. temporary nature of employment contract). Finally, topics 5 and 11 were linked to management (e.g., layoffs, downsize, restructure, organization, downturn) and employee relations (e.g., union/management, strategy, labor relations, wage). 7 The observed clustering of words does appear to provide some evidence that research on job insecurity has to some degree advanced in disciplinary silos. While this may not be entirely surprising, they also offer compelling intersections and avenues for future synergistic research. For example, there might be benefits from a cross-fertilization of organizational psychology's focus on developing rich models incorporating mediating and moderating mechanisms with occupational medicine's focus on objective health outcomes, such as cardiovascular disease, blood pressure, and other measurable physical health outcomes rather than self-reported health (as is more typically measured in organizational psychology studies). Similarly, a cross-fertilization between the economic topics (8 & 9) and the qualitative job insecurity topic 14 could spur interesting research into the impact of macro-and micro-economic labor market and employment contract policies on individual-level perceptions of deteriorating qualitative job insecurity.

Reinert Method. Figure 2 depicts the dendrogram obtained with the Reinert method, which should be read from top to bottom. Each bracket identifies a successive iteration of the algorithm. Upon reaching the final set of classes, Figure 2 also presents the 5 most commonly occurring words within each class; these can be used to identify themes (e.g., research foci, disciplinary approaches, methodologies, etc.). As can be seen, the first iteration (uppermost bracket) separated two distinct lines of investigation, which appeared to represent organizational psychology (encompassing the final classes 4, 5, 6, and 8, representing 54% of all abstracts) and economics, public health, and sociology (encompassing classes 1, 2, 3, and 7, representing 45% of all abstracts). These proportions alone are of interest, since they highlight the prolific job insecurity research being generated from nonorganizational psychology disciplines. On the other branch of the dendrogram, the algorithm first split occupational medicine (class 1, as evidenced by words such as oddsratio, cohort, logistic, and exposure) from economics (classes 2, 3, and 7) and then further separated macroeconomics and quantitative sociology (class 7 including words such as panel studies, labor market, household, unemployment) from political economics (class 3, e.g., trade, policy) and microeconomics (class 2, e.g., demographic, bargain, income). By visually analyzing the dendrogram and the words associated with each cluster, it is quite apparent that the investigation of job insecurity in the last forty years has occurred within clear and distinct disciplinary boundaries. Four broad areas may be identified, corresponding to organizational psychology, health (i.e., public health and occupational medicine), economics, and sociology. It is interesting to note that the algorithm split organizational psychology from the other fields immediately, signifying a stark contrast between the organizational psychology-related words and meanings (with clusters including focal words such as: job satisfaction, burnout, well-being, social exchange, commitment, performance, turnover) and those belonging to the other disciplines (where the clusters included focal words such as: panel, labor market, policy, microeconomic, bargain, odds ratio, cohort). As noted earlier, the Reinert method initially creates two classes such that the co-occuring words are most consistent within each class while at the same time being most different between each other. By examining the content of the word clusters within these branches, we can speculate reasons for this branching. However, just as a researcher conducting an exploratory factor analysis makes interpretive judgment calls on the meaning of the extracted factors (and other researchers may differ in their opinions as to the most appropriate labeling and meaning of those factors), this is equally true of our textual statistics analysis.

With that in mind, we offer several possible explanations for this branching pattern and the resultant apparent silos. First, this finding may reflect deep distinctions regarding the conceptualization of job insecurity itself. Whereas in the organizational psychology field, job insecurity is often understood and conceptualized as a subjective experience (Shoss, 2017) and scholars have argued that self-report measures may be the most appropriate way to validly and reliably measure such construct (Vander Elst et al., 2011), scholars in other disciplines have traditionally embraced a more objective measurement of job insecurity (Burgard, 2020), implying a correspondence between one's employment contract and level of job insecurity. However, this may be changing. For example, a recent review of epidemiological research on precarious employment (Benach et al., 2014) defined job insecurity as a "perceptual phenomenon" (p. 236), seemingly subscribing to a subjective view of the construct (see Blomqvist et al., 2020;LaMontagne et al., 2020 for other recent examples). Nevertheless, this initial branching may reflect historically differing conceptualizations and operationalizations of the job insecurity construct.

The observed disciplinary distinctions may also be a byproduct of differing prevalent methodological (e.g., "panel" studies in class 7) and statistical techniques germane to each field. For example, the most prevalent regression-based technique used in epidemiological research and econometrics is arguably binomial logistic regression (comporting with the words "odds ratio" and "logistic" in class 1). This implies that the outcome variable must have only two levels (i.e., secure vs. insecure), easily represented by one's contract type (e.g., permanent vs. contingent). On the other hand, organizational psychologists often measure constructs with approximately continuous items reflecting an assumed continuum and comporting with a different conceptualization of job insecurity. Moreover, organizational psychology research models often focus on identifying explanatory mechanisms (e.g., "mediation" in class 8) and boundary conditions (e.g., "moderation" in class 6), as well as explicitly derive hypotheses from theory (e.g., "job resources" and "social exchange" in class 8).

Finally, these topical distinctions may also stem from a focus on different predictors and correlates across the disciplines, as well as different levels of analysis that have been traditionally of interest in a specific field. For example, the field of organizational psychology has long been criticized for its almost exclusive focus on individual-level behaviors, despite the widespread knowledge that organizational phenomena take place at different levels (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000). This is further substantiated by the nearly exclusive consideration of individuallevel correlates of job insecurity investigated in recent meta-analytical studies. However, other disciplines such as sociology, public health, and economics hold that job insecurity may be determined, in large part, by macrolevel structures and processes (Muntaner et al., 2010), such as global labor markets (topic 8 in Table 1; classes 3 and 7 in Figure 2) and population health inequalities (topics 4 and 7 in Table 1; class 1 in Figure 2). Encouragingly, as discussed further below, this mainstream perspective within other non-OP disciplines has been adopted by a small but emerging stream of organizational psychology job insecurity research modeling contextual variables at the organizational, community, and societal levels (e.g., Debus et al., 2012;Probst et al., 2020). Nevertheless, these historical differences in examining job insecurity as a micro-individuallevel phenomenon, as opposed to a more macroand contextually-determined phenomenon may have also contributed to the initial branching of organizational psychology from the rest of the dendrogram.

Results from the full corpus LDA Approach. The corpus was comprised of 5156 unique words that occurred 269,270 times. We ran a series of LDA models with a maximum of 50 topics (in 5-unit increments) and inspected the change in resulting loglikelihood values. In line with Griffiths and Steyvers (2004), the loglikelihood curve showed that the optimal model of T = 15 is complex enough to accurately fit the information available without producing more parameters than can be justified by the data.

Table 1 depicts the topics and the top 20 representative words for each, ranked based on their highest posterior probability. Many of the identified topics reflected rather clear disciplinary boundaries. For instance, topics 3 (containing words focused on commonly studied outcomes of job insecurity, including job satisfaction, engagement, and commitment), 10 (containing words focusing on quantitative job insecurity: job loss, affect, subjective, threat, worry), and 14 (containing words commonly studied in relation to qualitative job insecurity, e.g., benefit, career, time, valued aspects) were related largely to organizational psychology. Topics 4 and 7 represented occupational medicine with clear foci on physical health outcomes of job insecurity (e.g., blood pressure, depression, cardiac disease). Topics 2 (e.g., wellbeing, life satisfaction, work-family conflict, partner, child, care) and 6 (e.g., burnout, safety, injury, accident underreporting) contained constructs (e.g., work-life balance and occupational safety) germane to the narrower sub-discipline of occupational health psychology. Topic 8 reflected research in macroeconomics with emerging key words such as labor market, wage, public/private, and economy; on the other hand, Topic 9 represented the microeconomic approach (e.g., with a focus on the nature of the permanent vs. temporary nature of employment contract). Finally, topics 5 and 11 were linked to management (e.g., layoffs, downsize, restructure, organization, downturn) and employee relations (e.g., union/management, strategy, labor relations, wage). 7 The observed clustering of words does appear to provide some evidence that research on job insecurity has to some degree advanced in disciplinary silos. While this may not be entirely surprising, they also offer compelling intersections and avenues for future synergistic research. For example, there might be benefits from a cross-fertilization of organizational psychology's focus on developing rich models incorporating mediating and moderating mechanisms with occupational medicine's focus on objective health outcomes, such as cardiovascular disease, blood pressure, and other measurable physical health outcomes rather than self-reported health (as is more typically measured in organizational psychology studies). Similarly, a cross-fertilization between the economic topics (8 & 9) and the qualitative job insecurity topic 14 could spur interesting research into the impact of macro-and micro-economic labor market and employment contract policies on individual-level perceptions of deteriorating qualitative job insecurity.

Reinert Method. Figure 2 depicts the dendrogram obtained with the Reinert method, which should be read from top to bottom. Each bracket identifies a successive iteration of the algorithm. Upon reaching the final set of classes, Figure 2 also presents the 5 most commonly occurring words within each class; these can be used to identify themes (e.g., research foci, disciplinary approaches, methodologies, etc.). As can be seen, the first iteration (uppermost bracket) separated two distinct lines of investigation, which appeared to represent organizational psychology (encompassing the final classes 4, 5, 6, and 8, representing 54% of all abstracts) and economics, public health, and sociology (encompassing classes 1, 2, 3, and 7, representing 45% of all abstracts). These proportions alone are of interest, since they highlight the prolific job insecurity research being generated from nonorganizational psychology disciplines. In a subsequent iteration, the organizational psychology cluster split into domains related to individual characteristics/constructs (classes 6 and 8 which contained words implying more employee-focused research, e.g., job satisfaction, well-being, burnout, engagement) versus

sections.paper_id: Bazzoli and Probst - 2022 - Taking stock and moving forward A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job i
paragraphs: Using the results from the LDA analysis of the full corpus, we next assigned each abstract to a topic based on the highest posterior probability of belonging to such LDA topic and split the abstracts assigned to the organizational psychology topics (i.e., LDA topics 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 11, and 14) from those assigned to other disciplines (i.e., topics 4, 7, 8, and 9), forming two sub-corpora. The abstracts originally assigned to noise topics (i.e., 1, 12, 13, and 15) were manually checked and re-assigned to the next most-probable non-noise topic. A majority (58%) of abstracts were assigned to the OP corpus and the remaining 42% were assigned to the non-OP corpus. These figures closely mirror the initial dendrogram partition generated by the Reinert method, providing some cross-validation evidence of the robustness of these methods and consistency of this apparent disciplinary divide.

Organizational Psychology Corpus. An inspection of the log-likelihood trajectory indicated that the most parsimonious model of T = 25 fits the data best. The 20 most likely words for each topic are reported in Table 2. As with our original LDA analysis on the full corpus, there were a few topics that contribute to the fit of the model but are "noise" for the purposes of our analysis. These topics (i.e., 6, 8, 21, and 22) reflect common terms in scientific papers and are therefore disregarded from further consideration. The remaining 21 topics are further described below.

One of the salient themes that seem to emerge is related to research methods and statistics. Specifically, regression-based approaches appear by far the most prevalent among the statistical tools used by scholars, including: multilevel models (topic 9; see e.g., Debus et al., 2012;Sora et al., 2010), structural equation modeling (topic 15; see e.g., Höge et al., 2015), and mediation analyses linked to OHP-focused research demonstrating adverse safety outcomes of job insecurity and mechanisms/mediating pathways to explain these relationships (topic 14; see e.g., Probst & Brubaker, 2001;Quinlan & Bohle, 2009). However, the LDA algorithm also identified a topic that seems to reflect more qualitative research (topic 24; see e.g., Astarlioglu et al., 2011;Hartley, 1998) on job insecurity. Despite this, it seems that organizational psychology research remains heavily quantitative, as confirmed by the number and breadth of topics specific to quantitative research methods. The most probable words for topics 15 and 18 (e.g., model, outcome, longitudinal and cross-sectional designs, results) were semantically close to quantitative methods. Related to this, topic 20 is associated with the covariates that are often included in quantitative analyses as control variables (see e.g., Näswall & De Witte, 2003). Additionally, related to the development of multilevel modeling (topic 9), the LDA analysis identified topics that seem to represent contextual variables both within (topics 4 and 13; see e.g., Jiang & Probst, 2016;Sora et al., 2013) and outside of (topic 1; see e.g., Debus et al., 2012) the organization. The goal of exploring relationships between constructs that reside at different levels and their nonindependence is the guiding principle of these lines of research (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000;Sinclair et al., 2010), and may represent emerging areas of cross-fertilization from economics, sociology, and public health disciplines which often consider more macro-level variables.

The LDA algorithm also identified a topic that seems to refer to the limited body of research done on positive outcomes of job insecurity with focal words including "opportunity" "develop" and "well-being" (topic 10; see e.g., Giunchi et al., 2019;Sjöberg, 2018). Finally, topics 12 and 16 appear to focus on early work conceptualizing job insecurity as an occupational stressor (see e.g., Mauno et al., 1999;Mohr, 2000) and the development of valid measures to operationalize the construct (see e.g., Probst, 2003;Vander Elst et al., 2014).

Analysis of Topic Trends over Time. In order to identify emerging ("hot") and declining ("cold") topics, based on their change in prevalence over the years, we conducted a simple trend analysis on the mean posterior θ by time (see Footnote 5). As can be seen in Figure 3, the trend analysis indicates topics 2, 5, 9, 10, 14, and 25, corresponding to multilevel modeling and contextual variables, affective job insecurity, safety outcomes, interventions, and the job demand-resources model, were considered "hot." On the other hand, topics 12, 16, and 17, corresponding to research on definitional and measurement aspects of job insecurity, as well as performance-related correlates, were considered "cold." Indeed, these areas exhibited an early flurry of activity starting in 1984 but quickly declining by the mid-1990s.

A visual analysis of Figure 3 suggests that the late 1990s and early 2000s were a period of change for job insecurity research, as many of the hot topics began to rise and several cold topics began to decline, compared to the early years. For ease of interpretation, we divided the temporal development of job insecurity research into three main periods: Early Years: Conceptualization andMeasurement (1984-2000), Developing Years: Methodological Sophistication (2000-2010), and Current Trends: OHP-Grounded Focus (2010-2019).

Below, based on identified hot and cold topics, we aim to trace a history of job insecurity research. In doing so, we embrace the assumption that job insecurity research was not (and still is not) independent from other similar lines of inquiry in the broader organizational psychology field (Cole, 1983).

Cold Topics. Kuhn (1970) argued that scientific fields progress through several stages of evolution. The early stage of science -often exploratory and atheoreticalcan only progress toward paradigmatic science through the development of measurement, identification of relevant theories, and application of rigorous (and agreed upon) methodology. Thus, not surprisingly, the early years of scholarly interest in job insecurity similarly focused on the conceptualization and measurement of this construct (or as Kuhn would say, building our "disciplinary matrix", Kuhn, 1970, p. 182). This is represented by topic 12 which includes early attempts to establish job insecurity as a psychological construct and identify its defining characteristics; topic 16 which included issues associated with its measurement; and topic 17 which began to examine its performance correlates.

Based on the trend analysis, after an early burst of scholarly activity (topic 12), organizational psychology scholars appeared to have reached a consensus on the major defining characteristics of job insecurity (De Witte et al., 2015). Following this early but necessary phase, the trend analysis reflects less focus on conceptualizing and defining job insecurity over time, despite recent exhortations by scholars (e.g., Shoss, 2017) that we still need to advance our conceptualization and measurement of job insecurity.

A further cornerstone of developing a research field involving a psychological construct is the operationalization of said construct (topic 16). Interestingly, three of the most widely-used scales to measure job insecurity, namely the Job Insecurity Scale (Ashford et al., 1989), another Job Insecurity Scale (De Witte, 2000), and the Job Security Index (Probst, 2003) were all developed before or during the early 2000s. Since then, interest in the measurement of job insecurity has been declining, perhaps because these scales have been largely accepted as ways of validly measuring the construct. 2 for words associated with each topic.

The last part of establishing a psychological construct is an investigation of its correlates. A focus on performance-based correlates (topic 17) may reflect an early interest in the occurrence of job insecurity under specific organizational conditions (e.g., downsizing, mergers, acquisitions) and the prevalence of the industrial/personnel side of organizational psychology field. Interestingly, a recent survey of organizational psychology scholars and practitioners highlighted a declining interest in such themes (Highhouse et al., 2020), along with declining impact factors for journals that publish mainly assessment and performance-related papers. Therefore, research on performance correlates could simply reflect the larger trend found within the organizational psychology field.

Nevertheless, while the trend analysis clearly shows that scholarly interest in these topics has declined in favor of Kuhnian "paradigmatic research" (i.e., theory-driven investigations resting upon the disciplinary matrix of agreed upon values and assumptions), it may be that the field of job insecurity research is poised to approach what Kuhn termed a "revolutionary phase" in the evolution of scientific inquiry. During such paradigmatic shifts, scientists question the established paradigm and assumptions. Within the job insecurity realm, this may involve a reexamination of what is meant by the construct of job insecurity itself. As we discuss later, part of this paradigm questioning may be prompted by shifts in the changing nature of work since the early origins of job insecurity research and conceptualization of this construct.

Hot Topics. Conversely, other topics have experienced a rise in scientific interest over the years and may be explained by the influence that other lines of inquiry have had on job insecurity research. It has long been held that organizational scholars residing in psychology departments show different academic interests than organizational scholars residing in business schools (Judge, 2003), and scholars have posited that eventually the latter will take over the former (Aguinis et al., 2014) due to a brain drain from psychology departments toward business schools. Management-related topics in job insecurity research (topic 2) showed a significant increase in scholarly interest over the years, even though the average posterior probability of the topic is arguably low. On the one hand, this seems to lend empirical evidence that business-related research on job insecurity is on the rise. Tellingly, research that attempts to understand the difference between cognitive and affective job insecurity (grounded in the social psychological concepts of cognition and affect; topic 5) has also exhibited increased scientific interest over time.

Topics 9 and 10 showed increased interest perhaps in response to developments in other fields of psychological inquiry. As seen in Figure 5, the focus on multilevel modeling (topic 9) began to rise in the late 1990s, likely in response to developments in statistics that resulted in a greater availability of programs that can easily estimate such models (e.g., Mplus was developed in 1998, HLM was widely used in the late 1990s). Likewise, the heightened interest in positive outcomes of job insecurity (topic 10; such as workers' wellbeing, work engagement, career development) may be due in part to the impact of the 1998 APA Presidential Address by Seligman, who introduced positive psychology to the American Psychological Association, which in turn spurred the development of new lines of research across the entire discipline. Interestingly, these topics increased the most between 2000 and 2010 and plateaued thereafter, which corresponds to the period we labeled as methodological sophistication. Although both multilevel modeling and positive psychology both had a sizeable impact when they were first proposed, it seems now that these streams of research are somewhat stagnant within the job insecurity field, even though the parent fields keep evolving.

During the approximate same timeframe, another branch of psychological science was developing, namely, occupational health psychology. Indeed, one of the discipline's flagship journals, Work & Stress, launched in 1987 and was later published in association with the European Academy of Occupational Health Psychology, while its North American counterpart, the Journal of Occupational Health Psychology, launched nine years later stemming from discussions in the early 1990s between the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) and the American Psychological Association. The line of research represented by topic 14 investigated the adverse effects of job insecurity on safety-related outcomes by means of establishing causal chains (i.e., mediation analysis). Interestingly, topic 14's scientific interest has been consistently rising, as opposed to multilevel modeling and positive psychology, which both show flatter slopes. This may reflect concerted and continuing efforts to bolster occupational health and safety research, as evidenced by NIOSH's funding of OHP graduate training programs, the establishment of the Total Worker Health initiative in the U.S., the International Labor Organization's Decent Work initiative, and the Healthy Workplaces Campaigns by EU-OSHA, as well as funding by such agencies to increase research linking psychosocial workplace stressors such as job insecurity to safety outcomes.

Last, topic 25, referring to resource theories, has exhibited a significant increase in scientific interest since the early 1990s. Before then, the most accredited stress theories were either psychophysiological or appraisal-based, while Hobfoll (1989) published his seminal paper arguing for the study of individual resources. Ten years later, another resource-based theory (the JD-R model; Bakker & Demerouti, 2017) was developed. Similar to the occupational health psychology topic ( 14), this topic has also seen steady incremental interest over the years, defining the last period of job insecurity research, i.e., Current Trends. Tellingly, resource theories are firmly grounded in the organizational psychology field, whereas OHP is linked to organizational psychology but also draws from different disciplines, such as occupational medicine, health psychology, and ergonomics.

The abstracts originally assigned to noise topics (i.e., 1, 12, 13, and 15) were manually checked and re-assigned to the next most-probable non-noise topic. A majority (58%) of abstracts were assigned to the OP corpus and the remaining 42% were assigned to the non-OP corpus. Organizational Psychology Corpus. An inspection of the log-likelihood trajectory indicated that the most parsimonious model of T = 25 fits the data best. The abstracts originally assigned to noise topics (i.e., 1, 12, 13, and 15) were manually checked and re-assigned to the next most-probable non-noise topic. A majority (58%) of abstracts were assigned to the OP corpus and the remaining 42% were assigned to the non-OP corpus. These figures closely mirror the initial dendrogram partition generated by the Reinert method, providing some cross-validation evidence of the robustness of these methods and consistency of this apparent disciplinary divide.

Organizational Psychology Corpus. An inspection of the log-likelihood trajectory indicated that the most parsimonious model of T = 25 fits the data best. The 20 most likely words for each topic are reported in Table 2. As with our original LDA analysis on the full corpus, there were a few topics that contribute to the fit of the model but are "noise" for the purposes of our analysis. These topics (i.e., 6, 8, 21, and 22) reflect common terms in scientific papers and are therefore disregarded from further consideration. The remaining 21 topics are further described below.

One of the salient themes that seem to emerge is related to research methods and statistics. Specifically, regression-based approaches appear by far the most prevalent among the statistical tools used by scholars, including: multilevel models (topic 9; see e.g., Debus et al., 2012;Sora et al., 2010), structural equation modeling (topic 15; see e.g., Höge et al., 2015), and mediation analyses linked to OHP-focused research demonstrating adverse safety outcomes of job insecurity and mechanisms/mediating pathways to explain these relationships (topic 14; see e.g., Probst & Brubaker, 2001;Quinlan & Bohle, 2009). However, the LDA algorithm also identified a topic that seems to reflect more qualitative research (topic 24; see e.g., Astarlioglu et al., 2011;Hartley, 1998) on job insecurity. Despite this, it seems that organizational psychology research remains heavily quantitative, as confirmed by the number and breadth of topics specific to quantitative research methods. The most probable words for topics 15 and 18 (e.g., model, outcome, longitudinal and cross-sectional designs, results) were semantically close to quantitative methods. Related to this, topic 20 is associated with the covariates that are often included in quantitative analyses as control variables (see e.g., Näswall & De Witte, 2003). Additionally, related to the development of multilevel modeling (topic 9), the LDA analysis identified topics that seem to represent contextual variables both within (topics 4 and 13; see e.g., Jiang & Probst, 2016;Sora et al., 2013) and outside of (topic 1; see e.g., Debus et al., 2012) the organization. The goal of exploring relationships between constructs that reside at different levels and their nonindependence is the guiding principle of these lines of research (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000;Sinclair et al., 2010), and may represent emerging areas of cross-fertilization from economics, sociology, and public health disciplines which often consider more macro-level variables.

Topics 5 and 19 hint at a focus on differentiating among different types of job insecurity, namely affective job insecurity (see e.g., Huang et al., 2012;Pienaar et al., 2013) and qualitative job insecurity. The former refers to employees' emotional reactions to threats to one's job (Probst, 2003), while the latter refers to the possibility of losing valued features of one's job (e.g., benefits, flexible hours, pay cut; Brondino et al., 2020;Hellgren et al., 1999). Interestingly, the LDA analysis did not reveal distinct topics focused specifically on cognitive job insecurity (referring to cognitive beliefs regarding threats to one's job) or quantitative job insecurity (referring to the possibility of being laid off, and thus losing their job as such). This may suggest that the latter conceptualizations may be so ubiquitous and assumed that they are not specified in the abstracts of job insecurity research. Alternatively, it might reflect that some conceptualizations and operationalizations of job insecurity include both aspects in their definitions and measures of job insecurity.

Scholars have also used a wide variety of theoretical foundations to explain the negative effects of job insecurity which become apparent when examining the topic groupings. One of the most prominent perspectives is the so-called stressor perspective (Sverke et al., 2002), which groups contributions based on resources theories (topic 25; Bakker & Demerouti, 2017;Hobfoll, 1989;see e.g., De Cuyper et al., 2012), and appraisal theory (topic 7; Lazarus & Folkman, 1984; see e.g., Bazzoli et al., 2021;Klandermans et al., 2010). While these theories predict differential outcomes, they share the assumption that job insecurity is a stressor. Another perspective is based on social exchange (topic 11; Ashford et al., 1989) and largely rooted in psychological contract theory (see e.g., De Cuyper & De Witte, 2006;Probst, Bazzoli, et al., posits that employees have expectations regarding what the employer should be providing, beyond a salary, in exchange for their labor (e.g., job security), and that the violation of such expectations leads to negative outcomes. The LDA algorithm also identified a topic that seems to refer to the limited body of research done on positive outcomes of job insecurity with focal words including "opportunity" "develop" and "well-being" (topic 10; see e.g., Giunchi et al., 2019;Sjöberg, 2018). Finally, topics 12 and 16 appear to focus on early work conceptualizing job insecurity as an occupational stressor (see e.g., Mauno et al., 1999;Mohr, 2000) and the development of valid measures to operationalize the construct (see e.g., Probst, 2003;Vander Elst et al., 2014).

Analysis of Topic Trends over Time. In order to identify emerging ("hot") and declining ("cold") topics, based on their change in prevalence over the years, we conducted a simple trend analysis on the mean posterior θ by time (see Footnote 5). As can be seen in Figure 3, the trend analysis indicates topics 2, 5, 9, 10, 14, and 25, corresponding to multilevel modeling and contextual variables, affective job insecurity, safety outcomes, interventions, and the job demand-resources model, were considered "hot." On the other hand, topics 12, 16, and 17, corresponding to research on definitional and measurement aspects of job insecurity, as well as performance-related correlates, were considered "cold." Indeed, these areas exhibited an early flurry of activity starting in 1984 but quickly declining by the mid-1990s.

A visual analysis of Figure 3 suggests that the late 1990s and early 2000s were a period of change for job insecurity research, as many of the hot topics began to rise and several cold topics began to decline, compared to the early years. For ease of interpretation, we divided the temporal development of job insecurity research into three main periods: Early Years: Conceptualization andMeasurement (1984-2000), Developing Years: Methodological Sophistication (2000-2010), and Current Trends: OHP-Grounded Focus (2010-2019).

Below, based on identified hot and cold topics, we aim to trace a history of job insecurity research. In doing so, we embrace the assumption that job insecurity research was not (and still is not) independent from other similar lines of inquiry in the broader organizational psychology field (Cole, 1983).

Cold Topics. Kuhn (1970) argued that scientific fields progress through several stages of evolution. The early stage of science -often exploratory and atheoreticalcan only progress toward paradigmatic science through the development of measurement, identification of relevant theories, and application of rigorous (and agreed upon) methodology. Thus, not surprisingly, the early years of scholarly interest in job insecurity similarly focused on the conceptualization and measurement of this construct (or as Kuhn would say, building our "disciplinary matrix", Kuhn, 1970, p. 182). This is represented by topic 12 which includes early attempts to establish job insecurity as a psychological construct and identify its defining characteristics; topic 16 which included issues associated with its measurement; and topic 17 which began to examine its performance correlates.

Based on the trend analysis, after an early burst of scholarly activity (topic 12), organizational psychology scholars appeared to have reached a consensus on the major defining characteristics of job insecurity (De Witte et al., 2015). Following this early but necessary phase, the trend analysis reflects less focus on conceptualizing and defining job insecurity over time, despite recent exhortations by scholars (e.g., Shoss, 2017) that we still need to advance our conceptualization and measurement of job insecurity.

A further cornerstone of developing a research field involving a psychological construct is the operationalization of said construct (topic 16). Interestingly, three of the most widely-used scales to measure job insecurity, namely the Job Insecurity Scale (Ashford et al., 1989), another Job Insecurity Scale (De Witte, 2000), and the Job Security Index (Probst, 2003) were all developed before or during the early 2000s. Since then, interest in the measurement of job insecurity has been declining, perhaps because these scales have been largely accepted as ways of validly measuring the construct. 2 for words associated with each topic.

The last part of establishing a psychological construct is an investigation of its correlates. A focus on performance-based correlates (topic 17) may reflect an early interest in the occurrence of job insecurity under specific organizational conditions (e.g., downsizing, mergers, acquisitions) and the prevalence of the industrial/personnel side of organizational psychology field. Interestingly, a recent survey of organizational psychology scholars and practitioners highlighted a declining interest in such themes (Highhouse et al., 2020), along with declining impact factors for journals that publish mainly assessment and performance-related papers. Therefore, research on performance correlates could simply reflect the larger trend found within the organizational psychology field.

Nevertheless, while the trend analysis clearly shows that scholarly interest in these topics has declined in favor of Kuhnian "paradigmatic research" (i.e., theory-driven investigations resting upon the disciplinary matrix of agreed upon values and assumptions), it may be that the field of job insecurity research is poised to approach what Kuhn termed a "revolutionary phase" in the evolution of scientific inquiry. During such paradigmatic shifts, scientists question the established paradigm and assumptions. Within the job insecurity realm, this may involve a reexamination of what is meant by the construct of job insecurity itself. As we discuss later, part of this paradigm questioning may be prompted by shifts in the changing nature of work since the early origins of job insecurity research and conceptualization of this construct.

Hot Topics. Conversely, other topics have experienced a rise in scientific interest over the years and may be explained by the influence that other lines of inquiry have had on job insecurity research. It has long been held that organizational scholars residing in psychology departments show different academic interests than organizational scholars residing in business schools (Judge, 2003), and scholars have posited that eventually the latter will take over the former (Aguinis et al., 2014) due to a brain drain from psychology departments toward business schools. Management-related topics in job insecurity research (topic 2) showed a significant increase in scholarly interest over the years, even though the average posterior probability of the topic is arguably low. On the one hand, this seems to lend empirical evidence that business-related research on job insecurity is on the rise. Tellingly, research that attempts to understand the difference between cognitive and affective job insecurity (grounded in the social psychological concepts of cognition and affect; topic 5) has also exhibited increased scientific interest over time.

Topics 9 and 10 showed increased interest perhaps in response to developments in other fields of psychological inquiry. As seen in Figure 5, the focus on multilevel modeling (topic 9) began to rise in the late 1990s, likely in response to developments in statistics that resulted in a greater availability of programs that can easily estimate such models (e.g., Mplus was developed in 1998, HLM was widely used in the late 1990s). Likewise, the heightened interest in positive outcomes of job insecurity (topic 10; such as workers' wellbeing, work engagement, career development) may be due in part to the impact of the 1998 APA Presidential Address by Seligman, who introduced positive psychology to the American Psychological Association, which in turn spurred the development of new lines of research across the entire discipline. Interestingly, these topics increased the most between 2000 and 2010 and plateaued thereafter, which corresponds to the period we labeled as methodological sophistication. Although both multilevel modeling and positive psychology both had a sizeable impact when they were first proposed, it seems now that these streams of research are somewhat stagnant within the job insecurity field, even though the parent fields keep evolving.

During the approximate same timeframe, another branch of psychological science was developing, namely, occupational health psychology. Indeed, one of the discipline's flagship journals, Work & Stress, launched in 1987 and was later published in association with the European Academy of Occupational Health Psychology, while its North American counterpart, the Journal of Occupational Health Psychology, launched nine years later stemming from discussions in the early 1990s between the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) and the American Psychological Association. The line of research represented by topic 14 investigated the adverse effects of job insecurity on safety-related outcomes by means of establishing causal chains (i.e., mediation analysis). Interestingly, topic 14's scientific interest has been consistently rising, as opposed to multilevel modeling and positive psychology, which both show flatter slopes. This may reflect concerted and continuing efforts to bolster occupational health and safety research, as evidenced by NIOSH's funding of OHP graduate training programs, the establishment of the Total Worker Health initiative in the U.S., the International Labor Organization's Decent Work initiative, and the Healthy Workplaces Campaigns by EU-OSHA, as well as funding by such agencies to increase research linking psychosocial workplace stressors such as job insecurity to safety outcomes.

Last, topic 25, referring to resource theories, has exhibited a significant increase in scientific interest since the early 1990s. Before then, the most accredited stress theories were either psychophysiological or appraisal-based, while Hobfoll (1989) published his seminal paper arguing for the study of individual resources. Ten years later, another resource-based theory (the JD-R model; Bakker & Demerouti, 2017) was developed. Similar to the occupational health psychology topic ( 14), this topic has also seen steady incremental interest over the years, defining the last period of job insecurity research, i.e., Current Trends. Tellingly, resource theories are firmly grounded in the organizational psychology field, whereas OHP is linked to organizational psychology but also draws from different disciplines, such as occupational medicine, health psychology, and ergonomics.

paragraphs: Corpus. An inspection of the log-likelihood trajectory indicated that the model with T = 15 fits the data best. The 20 most likely words for each topic are reported in Table 3. As with our original LDA analysis on the full corpus, there were a few topics that contribute to the fit of the model but are "noise" for the purposes of our analysis. These topics (i.e.,1, 13, and 15) reflect common terms in scientific papers and are therefore disregarded from further consideration.

Topics 9 and 14 seem to reflect research done within public health, which focused on health-related outcomes of job insecurity (e.g., anxiety and depressive symptoms, poor physical health; see e.g., Boya et al., 2008;Kim & von dem Knesebeck, 2016) as well as sex-based differences (see e.g., Ferrie et al., 2005;Kim et al., 2019). Topics 3 (with focal words such as: experience, gender, male, female, concern, response) and 12 (e.g., interview, semistructured, qualitative, account) seem to be associated with industrial sociology. Indeed, qualitative methods are commonly used in this discipline (see e.g., Hassard & Morris, 2018) and were leveraged to explore gendered understandings of job insecurity (see e.g., Williams, 2019), which is oftentimes linked to broader discourses at the societal level (see e.g., Nolan, 2009). Between these levels, scholars focused on labor relations and the relationship between employee and employer (topic 11) with emphasis on unions (see e.g., Bender & Sloane, 1999), wage disparities (see e.g., Bhandari & Heshmati, 2006) using larger datasets taken from institutional sources (topic 6; see e.g., Cazes & de Laiglesia, 2015) or qualitative methods (see e.g., Cadigan & Kirk, 2020). Topic 2 hints at macroeconomic themes such as country-level unemployment rates (see e.g., Burchell, 2011), labor market trends (see e.g., Garz, 2012), and comparison across countries (see e.g., Erlinghagen, 2008). It could be related to topic 6 (use of larger workforce datasets) and topic 8 (multilevel analyses; see e.g., Ellonen & Nätti, 2015;Erlinghagen, 2008). Topic 10 represents intervention research (e.g., intervention, control, experiment, effect size), which likely attempts to uncover feasible ways to reduce the negative impact of job insecurity on a host of outcomes using experimental research (see e.g., Barrech et al., 2018;Bussing, 1999). On the other hand, topic 4 captures prevalent statistical analyses used in correlational research (see e.g., Cheng et al., 2005;László et al., 2010). Topic 5 (with focal words including: life, social, family, relationship, stress) reflects research on the spillover effect of job insecurity on workers' life beyond their job (see e.g., Larson et al., 1994;Lim & Loo, 2003;Lim & Sng, 2006), which most of the time is carried out under the umbrella of family studies. Last, topic 7 points to the role of job insecurity as a threat to a decent and sustainable work (see e.g., Auer, 2006;Boyer, 2006;Duffy et al., 2019), which might lead to negative outcomes. Analysis of Topic Trends over Time. As with the organizational psychology corpus, we carried out a trend analysis. Our results showed that only topic 4 showed a positive slope (at the p < .001 level) and thus increased scholarly interest over time. As noted above, this topic reflects the use of correlational statistical techniques (e.g., regression and correlation). Although it is known that organizational psychology scholarship is for the most part quantitative, our previous results showed that the job insecurity field is not immune from this, and these results seem to suggest that job insecurity research done within other disciplines might be increasingly shifting towards correlational methods.

Interestingly, given that no other topics increased or decreased over time, this might suggest that these other disciplines have been far more stable in their focal areas of interest and methodologies used to investigate job insecurity. In part, this may be a function of the longer histories of these parent disciplines (i.e., economics, occupational medicine, public health, sociology) relative to organizational psychology. Thus, job insecurity researchers within these fields may have gained consensus regarding their disciplinary matrix well before scholars stemming from organizational psychology. On the other hand, it may also reflect overly entrenched disciplinary paradigms that are not as open to change and cross-fertilization from other disciplines compared to the analysis of the organizational psychology corpus. In other words, while research topics and trends within organizational psychology appear to have drawn from sociology, economics, and public health, the reverse may be less evident.

In other words, while research topics and trends within organizational psychology appear to have drawn from sociology, economics, and public health, the reverse may be less evident.

paragraphs: Job insecurity is a pervasive stressor that has been extensively investigated since the mid-1980s. Almost forty years later, investigating the development of job insecurity research across those years with the goal of identifying future steps was warranted. Below, we discuss how that research has progressed, and what our history (and potential gaps) might imply regarding how best the field can progress in the future and what organizational psychologists can learn from research done within other disciplines (and vice versa).
