paragraphs: Theme 1: Cautions Regarding Siloed Research. Our contribution seems to provide empirical evidence that some interactions between different disciplinary research traditions may occur, but those perhaps resemble "disinterested hostility" rather than "debates" (Burrell & Morgan, 1979, p. 36). Using two different approaches to textual analysis, we were able to clearly identify silos of knowledge representing four main disciplinary areas: the organizational psychology field (representing a slight majority of the job insecurity corpus, ∼55% of all abstracts), as well as public health, economics, and sociology (which accounted for the remaining corpus).

Nevertheless, there are some glimmers of cross-fertilization between these research traditions. For example, as noted earlier, there is increasing acceptance of job insecurity as a subjective phenomenon within the fields of public health and economics. Similarly, within organizational psychology, there has been growing recognition of the importance of incorporating contextual factors more typically studied within sociology and macroeconomics. Organizational psychology might further draw from public health and occupational medicine research by focusing on objective measures of health outcomes (e.g., cardiovascular events) rather than the current primary reliance on subjective assessments of psychosomatic symptomology. Fruitful cross-fertilization of ideas is useful and sometimes necessary for the development of the field, despite the highly specialized nature (e.g., methodology, epistemology, ontology, terminology) of disparate disciplinary approaches. In this spirit, our analysis tried to highlight both the contrasts and connections between disciplines in an empirical fashion and subsequently reflect upon the implications of the findings and the value-added by promoting paradigm crossing (Schultz & Hatch, 1996).

We also reiterate our earlier caution that other disciplinary fields may use different terms (e.g., "precarity") for what the organizational psychology field calls "job insecurity." By only including articles with the specific phrases of job (in)security and employment (in)security, our review of multidisciplinary research would have only captured articles that used like terms. As a result, our review arguably does not necessarily provide a truly comprehensive overview of the state of knowledge of job insecurity research. However, as noted earlier, we felt the risks of potentially conflating empirically and theoretically distinct constructs outweighed the risks associated with excluding such abstracts.

Theme 2: Methodological Sophistication. In many ways, the trend analysis of job insecurity research mirrors the rise and development of any field of scientific inquiry within the social sciences. Initially, researchers hotly debated a number of different aspects related to the appropriate conceptualization and operationalization of the job insecurity construct. In addition, there was a focus on fleshing out the basic nomological net to identify causes and consequences of job insecurity, as well as boundary conditions for these relationships. As the discipline developed some consensus in these areas, the next phase in the development of the field was characterized by increasing methodological sophistication. As argued by Hayes (2018), in order for a research area to mature, there needs to be a shift in focus from merely establishing that a relationship exists to explaining why it exists. Thus, increasingly, job insecurity research streams focused on testing proposed mediators as explanatory mechanisms, as well as articulating important contextual variables at the workgroup, organizational, industry, and national levels that could influence the strength and direction of the individual-level relationships. Of interest, it seems that organizational psychology scholars are more interested in testing theoretical models, whereas scholars in other disciplines tend to focus on determining the causal relationship between a known exposure, treatment, or policy and outcomes (e.g., using propensity scores, instrumental variables, differencein-differences, and other techniques to reduce the effect of confounding variables and reach a cleaner causal interpretation in observational studies). We believe that organizational scholars have much to learn from these approaches to strengthen their causal inference claims (see e.g., Bulbulia et al., 2021, for recommendations), and incorporating some of these techniques will undoubtedly benefit the field. At the same time, we discourage organizational psychology scholars from pursuing more complicated analyses only for complexity's sake (see Hambrick, 2007, for a defense of "simple" tests) or merely adding more variables (see Saylors & Trafimow, 2021, for a review of the risks associated with this), but instead invite them to reflect on what the most appropriate analytical technique is to answer their research questions. For instance, sometimes it makes sense to use a design correction (i.e., clusterrobust standard errors) instead of a full multilevel analysis when dealing with nested data (McNeish et al., 2017). With that, we would also like to encourage scholars to pay greater attention to transparency in their analytical choices (i.e., researcher degrees of freedom) and statistical model assumptions (e.g., assumptions on residuals, outcomes' distributional assumptions, etc.)

While acknowledging cautions regarding unnecessary complexity, there are numerous methodological advances that can be fruitfully implemented, in particular, to further our understanding of the temporal dynamics associated with the job insecurity phenomenon. This could include greater use of intensive longitudinal designs, such as latent growth curve modeling to explore parallel processes shaping the experience of job insecurity over time and/or latent transition analysis models to examine the effects of moving from one latent group to another. For example, a latent class analysis could identify latent (unobservable) subgroups of job insecure employees (Bazzoli et al., 2021b), whereas a latent transition analysis could use longitudinal data to predict movement among subgroups over time and/or consequences of such movement. Yet, we still as a field know relatively little of the dynamic nature of the job insecurity phenomenon and how it unfolds over time (Langerak et al., 2022). While the use of more advanced longitudinal designs and analytic techniques do not appear as major topic areas within our current textual statistics results, recent emerging research efforts (e.g., De Cuyper et al., 2019;Kinnunen et al., 2014;Klug et al., 2019;Mäkikangas et al., 2013) are encouraging.

Theme 3: Pushing Theoretical Boundaries? Considering the emergent topics through a theoretical lens, it appears that job insecurity research (within the organizational psychology corpus at least) has been dominated by theories of resources (e.g., Conservation of Resources, Hobfoll, 1989;and Job Demands-Resources, Bakker & Demerouti, 2017), appraisal (e.g., Lazarus & Folkman's, 1984, transactional model of stress), and social exchange (e.g., Probst, Bazzoli, et al., 2021). Interestingly, no clear theoretical foundations appeared in the non-psychology corpus.

While reliance on established theory is necessary for paradigmatic research, as Shoss (2017) aptly noted, many of these theoretical foundations make similar predictions regarding the impact of job insecurity (albeit due to potentially different mediating mechanisms). Yet, as Platt (1964) and others (e.g., Meehl, 1990) have noted, to move a field forward, we need to be able to derive competing hypotheses (i.e., utilize strong inference as our model of inquiry) to be able to better discern the value of these theories. We would add to that a call to consider alternative paradigmatic frameworks that might push our understanding of the nature of and processes involving job insecurity, such as uncertainty management (Brashers, 2001), scarcity theory (Mullainathan & Shafir, 2014), emotional contagion (Barsade & Knight, 2015), organizational justice (Colquitt, 2001), social identity theory (Selenko et al., 2017), and threat-rigidity theory (Staw et al., 1981).

Viewed from the perspective of other disciplines, organizational scholars' "theory fetish" (Hambrick, 2007(Hambrick, , p. 1346) is striking. Although we are not calling for organizational psychology scholars to abandon theory, which we believe has been foundational to the field's advancement, we suggest that scholars shed their misconception that hyper commitment to theory is the sole hallmark of a serious field. Slavish devotion to "theoretical contributions" can hinder authors' ability to publish papers that explore unexpected, relevant, fascinating, and important facets of job insecurity, but do not yet have an overarching theory that explains all the findings. Other disciplines (e.g., economics; Miller, 2007) rely on the reporting of important facts first, so that subsequent researchers can understand how and why. Additionally, many theories upon which job insecurity research rests seem to be somewhat interchangeable and perhaps redundant (i.e., they make comparable predictions): given that theories are lenses through which we construct our world, an unwavering commitment to certain theories will limit our ability to explore beyond what these theories already told us (i.e., observations are always theory-driven; Wittgenstein, 1953).

As noted earlier, we remain cautious with regards to completely abandoning theory (assuming this is ever possible in psychological sciences) or committing to fully data-driven approaches (e.g., machine learning and statistical learning theory). What we advocate for is more good theory: contrary to Sutton and Staw (1995) and several other voices in the field, we do not necessarily equate "good" with "empirically testable," but we would like to emphasize considering theories in terms of their generative capacity (Gergen, 1978(Gergen, , p. 1346)), that is, "challenging guiding assumptions of [our] culture, raising fundamental questions regarding contemporary social life, […] and furnishing new alternatives for social action."

sections.header_raw: Moving forward: a future research agenda
sections.header_norm: moving forward a future research agenda
paragraphs: If the current textual statistics analysis provides a map of where we have been, it might also suggest paths forward to where we might go. Below we discuss five such paths job insecurity researchers might embark upon.

Path 1: Insecure Jobs in a Changing Job Market. As the job market changes, our conceptualization of what constitutes job insecurity might also need to change. Based on our textual statistics analyses and prior literature review, current conceptualizations focus on the threat of job loss and/or loss of valued job features. Yet, such a conceptualization makes implicit assumptions about the nature of what a "job" is. With rise of the gig economy, zero-hour contracts, non-standard work arrangements, and crowdsourced tasks as a means of income, individuals are increasingly not employed by a single organization (whether it be full or parttime, contingent or permanent), but rather gain income via the completion of bidding for tasks and contracting their labor to numerous different organizations and clients (Lee et al., 2018). Friedman (2014) characterized these individuals as "workers without employers."

When the concept of job insecurity first arose in the mid-1980s, the vast majority of workers appeared on the payroll of an organization (Risher, 2020;Schaufeli, 2016). Today, estimates within the U.S. (MBO Partners, 2019) indicate that more than a quarter of all workers participate in the gig economy and >10% use it as their primary source of income. Disturbingly, only 32% of gig workers indicate that they have a regular source of income compared to 74% of traditional workers (MBO Partners, 2019). Similarly, zero-hour contract workers (sometimes referred to as casual contracts) are frequently seen in the UK and Australia and have no guarantees of a minimum number of work hours. Official estimates indicate that the proportion of UK workers under such zero-hour contracts quadrupled from 2011-2017 representing 3-4% of the UK workforce (Office of National Statistics, 2018). Within Australia, approximately 25% of workers are on casual contracts (Khadem, 2019). Surveys of such workers indicate many do not have the opportunity to work as many hours as they would like and experience denial of holiday pay, sick pay, and other expected benefits (Goodley & Inman, 2013). Others (e.g., trade unions) have raised concerns about the use of zero-hour contracts as a means of worker exploitation and reprisals, and have found that two-thirds of zero-hour workers would prefer to have jobs with guaranteed hours (TUC, 2017).

In such a job market, what does it mean for these workers to experience "job insecurity"? Might it be characterized by the lack of a steady and reliable source of income, lack of access to labor law protections, and a lack of benefits (e.g., health, retirement, social security, etc.), rather than the potential loss of the job itself? Might evaluating the outcomes of "precarious work" (more broadly defined than job insecurity) be more relevant to today's workforce? Relatedly, what is the relationship between qualitative and quantitative job insecurity for these workers? Do scholars need to expand the definition of qualitative job insecurity to include features that were previously considered standard but are now considered a benefit? As the nature of employment changes, our field's current conceptualization of job insecurity may need to also change, lest it become obsolete like many jobs of the past.

Path 2: Job Insecurity Beyond the Workplace. Thus far, the large majority of job insecurity research focused on organizational or individual outcomes. Very little is known about the influence of this stressor on people's behaviors outside their workplace. For example, a recent paper showed that job insecurity has a negative impact on employees' pro-environmental (i.e., "green") behaviors while on the job (Karatepe et al., 2020). Likewise, most research on pro-environmental behaviors done in organizational psychology focused on behaviors while at work (Dilchert & Ones, 2012). Hence, although efforts to promote green behaviors by organizations are laudable, they might not be effective if employees are job insecure.

In a similar fashion, job insecurity could affect other behaviors impacting the larger community in which employees live. For example, it can be argued that volunteerism (i.e., helping charities and lending one's time without an expectation of monetary compensation) could provide some meaning that has been taken away from job insecurity. Conversely, it might be hypothesized that insecure employees might withdraw from volunteering (Pavlova & Silbereisen, 2014; although see Selenko et al., 2020, as an example where no relationship was observed). Further, the impact of job insecurity on deviant and unethical behaviors (e.g., stealing) could extend to behaviors in the community. An ethnographic analysis in South Africa (Dickinson, 2015) found that people who had unstable jobs engaged in both community-building and community-damaging behaviors (e.g., fraud, lying, stealing). For instance, research has shown that in countries with a weaker safety net, employees tend to react to job insecurity more negatively (Debus et al., 2012), compared to employees living in a country with a more robust system.

Indeed, colleagues in the industrial sociology field have long held that most of organizational theories build on capitalist underpinnings and reinforce the current state of affairs involving the exploitation of workers. Although a growing minority of scholars advocate for such perspectives, they have not yet made their way into mainstream job insecurity research. We therefore call on organizational psychology scholars to leverage the wealth of knowledge that is generated by sociologists, economists, and critical scholars to better inform organizational psychology research. As an example, social exchange theory, which is one of the main theoretical frameworks in job insecurity (see our findings), relies on the assumption of meritocracy and harmonious social exchanges. However, scholars within and outside of organizational psychology have repeatedly found that these assumptions are not met (Bal & Dóci, 2018;Bazzoli & Probst, 2022a;Harvey, 2005) in today's workplaces. Nonetheless, social exchanges are featured prominently in job insecurity research (see e.g., Probst, Bazzoli, et al., 2021). Similarly, most organizational psychology research conceptualizes job insecurity as a value-neutral construct that is the result of companies' reaction to a globalized market (Budros, 1997). Yet, numerous perspectives suggested that job insecurity is used as a weapon to effectively control the workforce in and outside of the workplace (Anderson, 2017;Fleming, 2017). Interpretivist and constructionist approaches (Berger & Luckmann, 1966) to job insecurity are also worth exploring: abandoning the idea of a fully knowable and objective reality and instead stipulating that knowledge is socially co-constructed may open the door to novel lines of research that seek to elucidate people's lived experiences of job insecurity. For instance, the effects of labeling oneself as "job insecure" could be explored, analyzing what rhetorical artefacts and course of actions such label enhances and/or inhibits.

Although we are not persuaded that these critical stances will become widely accepted overnight, we invite organizational psychology scholars to reflect on the unintended consequences that reducing job insecurity to mere cognitive processes might have on obfuscating and ultimately reproducing the detrimental conditions that workers face every day. As a field, this may also cause us to perhaps question our heavy reliance on conceptualizing job insecurity primarily as a subjectively experienced phenomenon and instead reconsider the benefits of drawing from other disciplines which instead evaluate the outcomes associated with objective precarious employment conditions.

Path 4: Contributions of Qualitative Research Methods. Fourth, our analysis highlighted that the majority of job insecurity research has adopted a strictly quantitative approach. However, the algorithm also identified a topic representing qualitative approaches. We believe this encouraging sign of methodological diversity within our field should be further developed. Following Bartunek and Seo's (2002) call, we see potential growth in the field by focusing on qualitative research methods to explore job insecurity's nomological network to complement existing quantitative lines of research. Most importantly, these shared meanings attached to job insecurity may not exactly mirror known academic theories of job insecurity, opening up interesting possibilities for further exploration of how employees "make sense" of job insecurity.

Finally, like many other areas within organizational psychology (Ruggs et al., 2013), there is a dearth of research specifically addressing the prevalence and experience of job insecurity among underrepresented and marginalized worker populations. The contents of topic 20 indicate scholars often use age, race, gender, income, and education as control variables in their models. Yet, generally speaking, as a field, organizational psychology job insecurity researchers have not devoted significant attention to deeply considering the complex interplay among these variables, particularly with respect to race. If the current textual statistics analysis provides a map of where we have been, it might also suggest paths forward to where we might go. Below we discuss five such paths job insecurity researchers might embark upon.

Path 1: Insecure Jobs in a Changing Job Market. As the job market changes, our conceptualization of what constitutes job insecurity might also need to change. Based on our textual statistics analyses and prior literature review, current conceptualizations focus on the threat of job loss and/or loss of valued job features. Yet, such a conceptualization makes implicit assumptions about the nature of what a "job" is. With rise of the gig economy, zero-hour contracts, non-standard work arrangements, and crowdsourced tasks as a means of income, individuals are increasingly not employed by a single organization (whether it be full or parttime, contingent or permanent), but rather gain income via the completion of bidding for tasks and contracting their labor to numerous different organizations and clients (Lee et al., 2018). Friedman (2014) characterized these individuals as "workers without employers."

When the concept of job insecurity first arose in the mid-1980s, the vast majority of workers appeared on the payroll of an organization (Risher, 2020;Schaufeli, 2016). Today, estimates within the U.S. (MBO Partners, 2019) indicate that more than a quarter of all workers participate in the gig economy and >10% use it as their primary source of income. Disturbingly, only 32% of gig workers indicate that they have a regular source of income compared to 74% of traditional workers (MBO Partners, 2019). Similarly, zero-hour contract workers (sometimes referred to as casual contracts) are frequently seen in the UK and Australia and have no guarantees of a minimum number of work hours. Official estimates indicate that the proportion of UK workers under such zero-hour contracts quadrupled from 2011-2017 representing 3-4% of the UK workforce (Office of National Statistics, 2018). Within Australia, approximately 25% of workers are on casual contracts (Khadem, 2019). Surveys of such workers indicate many do not have the opportunity to work as many hours as they would like and experience denial of holiday pay, sick pay, and other expected benefits (Goodley & Inman, 2013). Others (e.g., trade unions) have raised concerns about the use of zero-hour contracts as a means of worker exploitation and reprisals, and have found that two-thirds of zero-hour workers would prefer to have jobs with guaranteed hours (TUC, 2017).

In such a job market, what does it mean for these workers to experience "job insecurity"? Might it be characterized by the lack of a steady and reliable source of income, lack of access to labor law protections, and a lack of benefits (e.g., health, retirement, social security, etc.), rather than the potential loss of the job itself? Might evaluating the outcomes of "precarious work" (more broadly defined than job insecurity) be more relevant to today's workforce? Relatedly, what is the relationship between qualitative and quantitative job insecurity for these workers? Do scholars need to expand the definition of qualitative job insecurity to include features that were previously considered standard but are now considered a benefit? sections.paper_id: Bazzoli and Probst - 2022 - Taking stock and moving forward A textual statistics approach to synthesizing four decades of job i
paragraphs: While there are numerous methods available to synthesize an accumulated body of literature, using a textual statistical approach allowed us to not only identify the dominant streams of job insecurity research being conducted within and outside of organizational psychology, but also examine how these research trends have changed over time. Due to the observed disciplinary silos, we advocate for job insecurity researchers (regardless of their disciplinary home) to be open to paradigmatic shifts in our conceptual understanding and investigation of job insecurity. We believe that such transformational shifts are likelier to occur when we are aware of trends and focal areas of research within and outside of our disciplinary matrix. Toward that end, we hope that our textual analysis of four decades of job insecurity research will prompt such crossfertilization and new insights.

sections.header_raw: Throughout this contribution, and in keeping with
sections.header_norm: throughout this contribution and in keeping with
paragraphs: Organizational Psychology Review's definition, we use the term "organizational psychology" to be broadly inclusive of applied, industrial, occupational, personnel, and work psychology. We also recognize the close disciplinary connection with organizational behavior; while scholars in the latter area tend to be housed in business schools, they share many theoretical, conceptual, and methodological disciplinary underpinnings with organizational psychology scholars, attend many of the same conferences, are often trained in organizational psychology programs, and publish in similar journals. 2. Following previous approaches to textual analysis both within and outside of the organizational psychology field, we included only abstracts in the analysis, as opposed to full-text manuscripts.

Including the full-text manuscript may have unforeseen and undesired effects; for instance, including the introduction section could result in a magnified impact of previously cited research, thus distorting the purpose of this contribution and potentially biasing the topic estimation. 3. We acknowledge that other disciplinary fields may have different terms for what the organizational psychology field calls "job insecurity." By only conducting searches for articles that included the specific phrases of job (in)security and employment (in)security, it is possible that we may have missed abstracts of studies that actually covered the topic of job insecurity as it has been conceptually and operationally defined within the organizational psychology literature, even if it is labeled differently (e.g., labor market security, work security, precarious employment). However, we believe that including such papers might lead to conceptual drift, particularly if abstracts were extracted where those terms were not intended to represent job insecurity as traditionally defined. 4. Whereas the LDA and the Reinert method share the same overall analytical goal, i.e. finding a relatively small set of topics/classes that accurately represents a corpus, their theoretical and computational approach is different. Both techniques are so-called bag-of-words techniques; that is, they approach abstracts as a figurative jar containing a number of words, whose order does not matter in the estimation process. Before the analysis, abstracts are in fact decomposed into a matrix that represents word occurrences in each abstract. However, the LDA analysis allows researchers to explore the topics' development over time, whereas the Reinert method is able to graphically represent the closeness/distance between clusters, aiding the researcher in making inferences about semantically closer clusters. For these reasons, we advocate for the use of both strategies. 5. The chosen Dirichlet parameters, and in particular δ, have an effect on the granularity of the model. Therefore, if we were to increase δ, we could expect to decrease T. These fixed parameters can be expected to produce a fine-grained model that accurately decompose the abstracts into latent topics that address rather specific macro research areas in the job insecurity literature in the first run and topics that address specific areas of job insecurity research within the OP literature. 6. For each abstract, the LDA algorithm computes the posterior probability that a topic is represented in an abstract. This is defined as the posterior theta (θ). Averaging the posterior theta values for each topic across years will lead to the values shown in the Y axis in Figure 3 (i.e., the mean posterior theta). Therefore, if that value is higher, it means that there is a higher average posterior probability of that topic is represented in papers published in that year. We considered topics as "hot" if their time slope was positive and statistically significant at the p < .001 level, and "cold" if their time slope was negative and statistically significant at the p < .001 level. 7. sections.text:
Organizational Psychology Review's definition, we use the term "organizational psychology" to be broadly inclusive of applied, industrial, occupational, personnel, and work psychology. We also recognize the close disciplinary connection with organizational behavior; while scholars in the latter area tend to be housed in business schools, they share many theoretical, conceptual, and methodological disciplinary underpinnings with organizational psychology scholars, attend many of the same conferences, are often trained in organizational psychology programs, and publish in similar journals. 2. Following previous approaches to textual analysis both within and outside of the organizational psychology field, we included only abstracts in the analysis, as opposed to full-text manuscripts.

Including the full-text manuscript may have unforeseen and undesired effects; for instance, including the introduction section could result in a magnified impact of previously cited research, thus distorting the purpose of this contribution and potentially biasing the topic estimation. 3. We acknowledge that other disciplinary fields may have different terms for what the organizational psychology field calls "job insecurity." By only conducting searches for articles that included the specific phrases of job (in)security and employment (in)security, it is possible that we may have missed abstracts of studies that actually covered the topic of job insecurity as it has been conceptually and operationally defined within the organizational psychology literature, even if it is labeled differently (e.g., labor market security, work security, precarious employment). However, we believe that including such papers might lead to conceptual drift, particularly if abstracts were extracted where those terms were not intended to represent job insecurity as traditionally defined. 4. Whereas the LDA and the Reinert method share the same overall analytical goal, i.e. finding a relatively small set of topics/classes that accurately represents a corpus, their theoretical and computational approach is different. Both techniques are so-called bag-of-words techniques; that is, they approach abstracts as a figurative jar containing a number of words, whose order does not matter in the estimation process. Before the analysis, abstracts are in fact decomposed into a matrix that represents word occurrences in each abstract. However, the LDA analysis allows researchers to explore the topics' development over time, whereas the Reinert method is able to graphically represent the closeness/distance between clusters, aiding the researcher in making inferences about semantically closer clusters. For these reasons, we advocate for the use of both strategies. 5. The chosen Dirichlet parameters, and in particular δ, have an effect on the granularity of the model. Therefore, if we were to increase δ, we could expect to decrease T. These fixed parameters can be expected to produce a fine-grained model that accurately decompose the abstracts into latent topics that address rather specific macro research areas in the job insecurity literature in the first run and topics that address specific areas of job insecurity research within the OP literature. 6. For each abstract, the LDA algorithm computes the posterior probability that a topic is represented in an abstract. This is defined as the posterior theta (θ). Averaging the posterior theta values for each topic across years will lead to the values shown in the Y axis in Figure 3 (i.e., the mean posterior theta). Therefore, if that value is higher, it means that there is a higher average posterior probability of that topic is represented in papers published in that year. We considered topics as "hot" if their time slope was positive and statistically significant at the p < .001 level, and "cold" if their time slope was negative and statistically significant at the p < .001 level. 7. The remaining several topics (1, 12, 13, and 15) consisted of words that tend to recur in scientific communication (e.g., hypothesis, model, finding, data, predict) but are not unique to our focal research interest. Although these topics contribute to the fit of the model, they represent noise for the purposes of this review and therefore were discarded from further theoretical consideration. 8. These are noted in parentheses and are preceded by the "see e.g.," notation.
